main_actor: Ubaid-period settlers of the southern alluvial plain
description: Eridu (modern Tell Abu Shahrain) was established on the edge of a freshwater marsh in southern Mesopotamia, making it one of the earliest known urban settlements in the world. Eighteen successive temple levels atop one another document continuous ritual activity spanning over two millennia. Sumerian tradition later called Eridu the first city where kingship descended from heaven (Sumerian King List). The site shows the transition from Ubaid village to proto-urban center with specialised craft production and long-distance trade in obsidian and bitumen.
ethical_notes: The date is derived from radiocarbon analysis of the earliest occupation levels (Ubaid 1). The Sumerian King List's claim of Eridu as 'first city' reflects Sumerian cosmology, not objective chronology. Other sites (Uruk, Tell al-'Ubaid) have competing early dates. Archaeological dating of this period carries inherent uncertainty of several centuries.

sources.citation: Safar, F., Mustafa, M.A. & Lloyd, S. (1981). Eridu. State Organization of Antiquities and Heritage, Baghdad.
Jacobsen, T. (1939). The Sumerian King List. Assyriological Studies 11, University of Chicago.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Date based on radiocarbon calibration of Ubaid 1 pottery levels. Margin of error +/- 200 years. No contemporary written records exist for this period.

main_actor: Neolithic agrarian community of the Konya Plain
description: Catalhoyuk was a large Neolithic proto-city on the Konya Plain of central Anatolia, housing an estimated 3,000-8,000 inhabitants at its peak. The settlement had no streets; houses were entered through roof openings. Walls were decorated with elaborate murals depicting hunting scenes, geometric patterns, and what may be the earliest known landscape painting (showing the settlement with an erupting Hasan Dag volcano). Inhabitants practiced mixed agriculture (wheat, barley, lentils) with continued hunting of aurochs and deer. Burials beneath house floors included rich grave goods of obsidian mirrors and shell beads. The site shows no evidence of centralised political authority or monumental architecture, suggesting an egalitarian social structure.
ethical_notes: Catalhoyuk is one of the most extensively excavated Neolithic sites (Mellaart 1961-1965, Hodder 1993-2018). Earlier interpretations of a 'Mother Goddess' cult (Mellaart) have been substantially revised by Hodder's excavations. The egalitarian interpretation is debated; absence of evidence for hierarchy is not evidence of absence. Population estimates vary widely depending on methodology.

sources.citation: Hodder, I. (2006). The Leopard's Tale: Revealing the Mysteries of Catalhoyuk. Thames & Hudson.
Mellaart, J. (1967). Catal Huyuk: A Neolithic Town in Anatolia. Thames & Hudson.

entity_links.notes: The Neolithic settlement of Catalhoyuk on the Konya Plain

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Dates from radiocarbon calibration of occupation layers. The settlement spans roughly 7500-5700 BCE (East mound) with the West mound continuing to ~5500 BCE. Margin of error +/- 100-200 years.

main_actor: Pre-Pottery Neolithic communities of the Fertile Crescent
description: The Neolithic Agricultural Revolution in the Fertile Crescent saw the independent domestication of emmer wheat, einkorn wheat, barley, lentils, peas, flax, chickpeas, sheep, goats, cattle, and pigs over a period of roughly two millennia. Key sites include Tell Abu Hureyra (Syria), Gobekli Tepe (southeast Turkey), Chogha Golan (Iran), and Jericho (Palestine). This transformation from mobile foraging to sedentary farming fundamentally altered human societies: population densities increased, social stratification emerged, and the epidemiological burden shifted from trauma to infectious disease as humans lived in proximity to livestock. The process was neither instantaneous nor universal; some communities adopted agriculture partially while maintaining foraging.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The term 'revolution' is a historiographic convenience; the process was gradual and polycentric. The narrative of agriculture as 'progress' has been challenged (e.g., James C. Scott, Against the Grain): early farmers were shorter, more disease-prone, and worked longer hours than foragers. Multiple independent centres of domestication existed worldwide (China, Mesoamerica, Sahel) -- presenting the Fertile Crescent as THE origin of agriculture reflects a Eurasian-centric historiography. We document this as the earliest well-attested case, not the only one.

sources.citation: Zeder, M.A. (2011). The Origins of Agriculture in the Near East. Current Anthropology 52(S4), S221-S235.
Fuller, D.Q. et al. (2014). Convergent evolution and parallelism in plant domestication. Trends in Ecology & Evolution 29(12), 727-735.
Scott, J.C. (2017). Against the Grain: A Deep History of the Earliest States. Yale University Press.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The 9000-7000 BCE range represents the main domestication window based on archaeobotanical and zooarchaeological evidence. Individual crop/animal domestication dates vary and are continually revised as new radiocarbon data emerge. Margin of error +/- 500 years for earliest phases.

main_actor: Temple administrators and scribes of Uruk (Eanna precinct)
description: The earliest known writing system emerged in the city of Uruk during the Late Uruk period (Uruk IV-III), initially as pictographic tokens and clay bullae used for accounting in the Eanna temple complex. The proto-cuneiform script on clay tablets recorded economic transactions: quantities of grain, livestock, and labour. By Uruk III (~3100 BCE), the system had evolved toward phonetic representation, enabling it to record language rather than just quantities. Over 5,000 archaic tablets have been recovered from Uruk. The invention appears linked to the complexity of redistributive temple economies rather than to a single inventor or royal decree.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Whether Uruk writing is truly the 'earliest' is debated. Egyptian hieroglyphs (Abydos tomb U-j, ~3250 BCE) and the undeciphered Indus script (~2600 BCE) represent independent traditions. The Vinca symbols (~5500 BCE) and Chinese Jiahu symbols (~6600 BCE) may be proto-writing but are not universally accepted as writing systems. Privileging cuneiform as 'first' reflects the state of current evidence, not a definitive judgement.

sources.citation: Nissen, H.J., Damerow, P. & Englund, R.K. (1993). Archaic Bookkeeping: Early Writing and Techniques of Economic Administration. University of Chicago Press.
Englund, R.K. (1998). Texts from the Late Uruk Period. In Bauer, J., Englund, R.K. & Krebernik, M. (eds.), Mesopotamien: Spaturuk-Zeit und Fruhdynastische Zeit. OBO 160/1.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The date range 3400-3100 BCE is based on archaeological stratigraphy and radiocarbon dating of Uruk IV-III levels. The transition from proto-writing to true writing is gradual and the boundary is definitional. Margin of error +/- 100 years.

location_name: Belovode / Plocnik, Vinca culture, modern Serbia; also Timna, southern Levant
main_actor: Vinca culture metalworkers (Balkans) and Chalcolithic communities of the eastern Mediterranean
description: The earliest known smelting of copper ore (as distinct from cold-working native copper) is attested at the Vinca culture site of Belovode in modern Serbia, dated to approximately 5000 BCE. Independently, copper smelting developed in Anatolia (Cayonu, Catal Hoyuk region), the southern Levant (Timna Valley), and Iran (Tal-e Iblis). The technology required achieving temperatures above 1,085 C in controlled reducing atmospheres, representing a fundamental breakthrough in pyrotechnology. Smelted copper enabled production of tools, weapons, and ornaments that could not be made from native copper alone, and it catalysed long-distance trade networks for ore and finished goods.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Copper smelting was independently invented in multiple regions. Presenting any single site as 'the' origin is misleading. Belovode currently has the earliest securely dated smelting evidence, but ongoing excavations in Anatolia and Iran may push back dates. The Eurasian focus of this entry reflects excavation history, not necessarily the actual distribution of early metallurgy.

sources.citation: Radivojevic, M. et al. (2010). On the origins of extractive metallurgy: new evidence from Europe. Journal of Archaeological Science 37(11), 2775-2787.
Roberts, B.W., Thornton, C.P. & Pigott, V.C. (2009). Development of metallurgy in Eurasia. Antiquity 83, 1012-1022.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Date based on radiocarbon calibration of Belovode smelting slag contexts. The 5000-4500 BCE range covers the earliest secure evidence; earlier claims exist but are debated. Margin of error +/- 200 years.

location_name: Choga Mami / Samarra region, central Mesopotamia
main_actor: Samarran culture communities of central Mesopotamia
description: The earliest known large-scale irrigation canals appear in the Samarra culture region of central Mesopotamia, at sites including Choga Mami and Tell es-Sawwan. These canals diverted water from the Tigris tributaries to fields that would otherwise have been too arid for rain-fed agriculture. Canal lengths of up to 3 kilometres have been traced archaeologically. This hydraulic technology enabled settlement of the alluvial plains south of the rainfall line (~200mm isohyet), opening the region where Sumerian civilisation would later develop. The labour coordination required for canal maintenance may have catalysed early forms of social hierarchy.
ethical_notes: The 'hydraulic hypothesis' (Wittfogel) linking irrigation to despotism is an oversimplification now largely rejected. Early irrigation does not require centralised authority; many small-scale cooperative systems are documented ethnographically. The date represents the earliest canal evidence in Mesopotamia; independent irrigation developed in many world regions.

sources.citation: Oates, J. & Oates, D. (1976). Early irrigation agriculture in Mesopotamia. In de G. Sieveking, G. et al. (eds.), Problems in Economic and Social Archaeology. Duckworth.
Helbaek, H. (1972). Samarran irrigation agriculture at Choga Mami in Iraq. Iraq 34(1), 35-48.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Date based on radiocarbon dating of Samarran occupation levels at Choga Mami. The technology likely developed gradually; the date represents the earliest archaeological attestation. Margin of error +/- 300 years.

location_name: Gobekli Tepe, Sanliurfa Province, southeast Anatolia
main_actor: Pre-Pottery Neolithic A hunter-gatherer communities of the upper Euphrates region
description: Gobekli Tepe is a monumental stone sanctuary consisting of multiple circular enclosures with T-shaped limestone pillars weighing up to 10 tonnes, carved with elaborate relief sculptures of animals (foxes, boars, snakes, vultures, aurochs). Built by pre-agricultural hunter-gatherer groups, the site challenges the assumption that monumental architecture requires settled agricultural societies. No evidence of permanent habitation has been found; the site appears to have been a ritual gathering place for mobile communities from a wide region. The construction required coordinated labour of hundreds of people, implying social organisation exceeding simple band-level society. The site was deliberately backfilled with refuse around 8000 BCE.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Gobekli Tepe has been subject to sensationalist claims ('oldest temple', 'rewrites history'). The excavator Klaus Schmidt's interpretation of a purely ritual site has been nuanced by ongoing excavations (German Archaeological Institute, since 2019) that have found evidence of domestic activities nearby. The pillars' iconography resists definitive interpretation without textual records. Only ~5% of the site has been excavated.

sources.citation: Schmidt, K. (2006). Sie bauten die ersten Tempel: Das ratselharte Heiligtum der Steinzeitjager. C.H. Beck.
Dietrich, O. et al. (2019). Cereal processing at Early Neolithic Gobekli Tepe, southeastern Turkey. PLoS ONE 14(5), e0215214.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Dates from radiocarbon calibration of organic material in pillar fill and bedrock cuttings. Layer III (oldest circular enclosures) dates to ~9500-8500 BCE; Layer II (smaller rectangular rooms) to ~8500-8000 BCE. Margin of error +/- 200 years.

main_actor: Narmer / Menes and the Upper Egyptian polity
description: The unification of Upper and Lower Egypt under a single ruler marks the traditional beginning of the First Dynasty and the Egyptian state. The Narmer Palette (found at Hierakonpolis) depicts a ruler wearing the crowns of both Upper and Lower Egypt, smiting enemies. Whether this represents a single military conquest or a gradual political consolidation over generations (encompassing rulers like Scorpion I/II, Ka, Narmer) is debated. The identification of Narmer with the legendary Menes (first king in Manetho's dynastic lists) is probable but not certain. The new state established its capital at Memphis (Ineb-Hedj, 'White Walls') at the apex of the Nile Delta.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Narmer Palette shows graphic violence (decapitated enemies, a king grasping a prisoner by the hair). State formation in early Egypt involved military coercion and the subjugation of Lower Egyptian polities. The 'unification' narrative in Egyptian sources presents this as a civilising act; the Lower Egyptian perspective is largely lost. The identification Narmer=Menes is a scholarly construct, not a proven fact.

sources.citation: Wilkinson, T.A.H. (1999). Early Dynastic Egypt. Routledge.
Narmer Palette, Egyptian Museum Cairo JE 32169 / CG 14716 (primary artifact, ~3100 BCE).

entity_links.notes: Unification traditionally marks the beginning of dynastic Kemet (Egypt)

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. The date ~3100 BCE is conventional and based on correlation of archaeological evidence with Manetho's king lists and radiocarbon dating. Competing chronologies place it between 3150-3050 BCE. The Egyptian calendar was not yet standardised at this period.

main_actor: Pre-Pottery Neolithic A (PPNA) community of the Jordan Valley
description: The settlement at Tell es-Sultan (ancient Jericho) is one of the earliest known continuously inhabited sites, with PPNA occupation dating to approximately 8000 BCE. The community built a massive stone tower (8.5m high, internal staircase) and a surrounding wall, representing some of the oldest known monumental architecture. The population is estimated at 2,000-3,000 inhabitants. The site exploited the perennial spring of Ain es-Sultan and developed early cultivation of wild cereals. Later PPNB levels show plastered skulls with modelled features, suggesting ancestor veneration practices. Whether the tower and wall served defensive, flood-control, or ritual purposes remains debated.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Jericho is a politically sensitive site in the occupied West Bank (Palestinian Territory). The archaeological site (Tell es-Sultan) has been excavated primarily by British (Kenyon) and Italian-Palestinian teams. Biblical narratives of Jericho's conquest (Joshua) are not supported by archaeology for the Late Bronze Age; the walls famously 'tumbled down' are from the Early Bronze Age, ~2000 years earlier. We document the archaeological evidence independent of religious narratives.

sources.citation: Kenyon, K.M. (1957). Digging Up Jericho. Ernest Benn.
Bar-Yosef, O. (1986). The Walls of Jericho: An Alternative Interpretation. Current Anthropology 27(2), 157-162.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Radiocarbon dates from PPNA levels. The tower has been dated to ~8300-7800 BCE. Continuous occupation makes precise founding dates impossible; 8000 BCE is conventional for the PPNA settlement phase. Margin of error +/- 300 years.

location_name: Kuk Swamp, Wahgi Valley, Western Highlands, Papua New Guinea
main_actor: Early Holocene highland communities of the Wahgi Valley
description: Kuk Swamp in the Wahgi Valley of Papua New Guinea provides the earliest known evidence of independent plant cultivation in the Australasia-Pacific region. Archaeological phases show deliberate drainage and planting mounds dated to approximately 7000-6500 BCE (Phase 1), with banana (Musa spp.), taro (Colocasia esculenta), and yam (Dioscorea spp.) among the cultivated species. This represents an independent origin of agriculture entirely separate from the Near Eastern, Chinese, and Mesoamerican centres. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2008. Later phases show increasingly sophisticated drainage systems culminating in gridded field systems by ~4000 BCE.
ethical_notes: ETHICS: Kuk Swamp demonstrates that agriculture was independently invented in the New Guinea highlands, challenging the long-standing Eurocentric narrative that agriculture spread from a single origin in the Fertile Crescent. The contribution of Melanesian peoples to global agricultural history is systematically underrepresented in standard textbooks. The Tok Pisin name reflects that the site is in modern Papua New Guinea; the original language of the ancient cultivators is unknown.

sources.citation: Denham, T.P. et al. (2003). Origins of Agriculture at Kuk Swamp in the Highlands of New Guinea. Science 301(5630), 189-193.
UNESCO World Heritage Centre (2008). Kuk Early Agricultural Site. Nomination File 887rev.

calendar_note: Proleptic Gregorian. Dates from radiocarbon calibration of organic material in palaeo-channel and mound fill (Phase 1). Some scholars date earliest cultivation to ~10,000 BP (~8000 BCE) based on pollen evidence, but the drainage-mound evidence is securely dated to ~7000-6500 BCE. Margin of error +/- 300 years.
